reasoning: Assessed against CEFR conversation descriptors across five dimensions. Oksana demonstrates solid A1 competence in familiar transactional contexts (shopping, personal information) with clear A1-to-A2 boundary features emerging. Her vocabulary range covers basic concrete needs; grammatical accuracy shows systematic gaps in verb tense marking, articles, plurals, and 3rd person -s that are characteristic of early A1. Interaction patterns (frequent hesitation, L1 resort, formulaic chunks) are consistent with A1. The strongest evidence for A1 rather than pre-A1 is her ability to complete multi-turn transactional dialogues and self-correct when prompted. The strongest evidence against A2 is the systematic absence of past tense marking and article use.
suggestions: Focus next 2-3 lessons on past simple (regular -ed and high-frequency irregulars: went, bought, cooked, had). Use weekend narration as recurring warm-up to drill naturally.
Introduce article practice through the food/shopping context already established. "The bread" (specific), "a tomato" (one of many), "some milk" (uncountable) as a triad.
Build 3rd person -s awareness through describing family routines: "She likes...", "He wants...", "It costs...". Connect to shopping context ("The milk costs...").
Continue role-play approach - student showed dramatic improvement from first to third attempt. Increase complexity gradually (asking for directions next, as student requested).
Assign listening homework - student identified fast speech as main real-world barrier [03:50].
When talking about yesterday or last week, always use past forms: "I went", "I bought", "I cooked" (not "I go", "I buy", "I cook"). Practice saying 5 past sentences each evening.
Before a noun, think: can I count it? Yes = use "a" or "an". No = use "some". Practice: "an apple", "a tomato", "some milk", "some bread".
For he/she/it, add -s to the verb: "she likes", "he wants", "it costs". Practice with family descriptions: "My daughter likes...", "My son wants..."
After numbers, always add -s: "two pounds", "three apples", "four tomatoes".
Visit a shop this week and use: "Can I have... please?", "How much is it?", "Sorry, can you speak slowly please?"
